notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
For a single renter with a Microsoft salary and no dependents, your income is your most valuable asset — and that shapes exactly how to prioritize your insurance coverage.
Since insurance decisions involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a licensed insurance professional for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Priority 1: Disability insurance
This is your highest priority by a significant margin. If you can't work, your paycheck stops — and with no partner income or dependents relying on you, you are the only safety net for yourself.
Microsoft typically offers group long-term disability (LTD) coverage, but employer plans commonly have limitations worth examining:
Benefit cap: Many group plans cap monthly benefits at $10,000–$15,000/month, which may fall short on a senior tech salary
Definition of disability: Group plans often use "any occupation" after 24 months, meaning benefits stop if you can qualify for any job — not just your current one
Portability: Employer group coverage typically ends if you leave Microsoft
Taxability: If Microsoft pays the premiums, your benefits are likely taxable, which erodes the effective replacement rate
Individuals commonly evaluate supplemental individual LTD policies that offer own-occupation definitions, benefit periods to age 65, and a 90-day elimination period (assuming a modest emergency fund covers that window).
Priority 2: Renters insurance with personal liability
This is inexpensive and addresses real, everyday risk — guest injuries, accidental property damage, theft of electronics and gear. Given your Robinhood brokerage and likely growing asset base, liability exposure matters.
Individuals with a professional income and growing savings commonly consider liability limits in the $300,000–$500,000 range rather than the bare minimum $100,000. Personal property coverage of $25,000–$50,000 is a common illustrative range for tech professionals with electronics and equipment — though a licensed agent can assess your specific belongings.
This range is illustrative only — not a recommendation.
Priority 3: Umbrella insurance
Once your renters liability is in place, umbrella coverage becomes the logical next layer — particularly as your Robinhood brokerage and Microsoft 401(k) grow. A $1M umbrella policy is commonly the starting point individuals consider when their assets begin accumulating meaningfully.
One practical note: umbrella policies typically require minimum underlying limits on your renters policy (often $300,000 personal liability) before you can qualify.
Priority 4: Life insurance
With no dependents and no co-signed debt visible in your profile, life insurance is your lowest priority today. The main scenarios where it becomes relevant for someone in your situation are covering final expenses, paying off any co-signed obligations, or locking in low premiums while young and healthy in anticipation of future dependents.
If that last scenario resonates — meaning you expect a partner or children within 10–15 years — locking in a level term policy now could make sense to explore with a licensed agent, since health changes over time can affect insurability.
Would you like to dig deeper into what to look for when reviewing Microsoft's existing group LTD plan against individual supplemental coverage options?

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.

$10K
